narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 28, a middle-class family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 76.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants most likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. The two biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your low year-three school decile ranking, which had the strongest negative influence compared to typical students who rank much higher on average, and your low year-one decile, which also significantly weighed against you relative to the dataset average around the middle of the pack. Your undergraduate GPA actually nudged things slightly in a positive direction, performing a bit below the average but still helping your case more than hurting it. Your LSAT score and family income had smaller influences tilting toward higher failure risk, while your full-time enrollment plan offered a minor positive boost, as most successful students in the data were full-time like you. Overall, your academic decile rankings stood out as much weaker than those of typical admitted students, who tend to cluster in higher deciles with stronger GPAs and LSATs. While this decision stings, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive academic environments, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your grades to climb those school deciles, retake the LSAT for a higher score, and perhaps seek targeted tutoring or mentorship programs designed for underrepresented students aiming for law. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we encourage you to reapply after addressing these key areas. Keep pushing forward; your perseverance will shine through.
